replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You won't have to modify a thing. Proclus Diadochus, the last great Pagan Neoplatonist, is recorded as having kept the holy days in the Athenian and Egyptian calendars; that implies, in the context of the times, that he invoked the Greek and Egyptian gods, each on their proper days and with appropriate rites. Since you're not mixing pantheons within a specific practice, you're fine.

Good Evening!

First, to share: Okay, this one is not directly magical, but might be of interest to some folks here, especially if you have any taste for English etymology, Linguistics in general, or things Germanic. For a while now, I've rather enjoyed English words that are derived from Germanic roots rather than French, Latin, Greek, or others. Turns out I'm not alone in this! At it's strongest, this is called "Linguistic Purism in English" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linguistic_purism_in_English), and some folks go so far as to characterize all of the French, Latin, Greek, and other-derived words as "bad" or "wrong" English and seek to purge them from the language. This seems to me like a fools' errand, but making a conscious choice to prefer Germanic-derived words over their French or Classical competitors is a fun creative constraint. Some fun examples of this taken to varying levels of seriousness are Poul Anderson's essay "Uncleftish Beholding" (https://groups.google.com/g/alt.language.artificial/c/ZL4e3fD7eW0/m/_7p8bKwLJWkJ?pli=1), his short story "The Tale of Hauk" (published in Swords Against Darkness), Lee Hollander's translation of the Poetic Edda, or Tolkien's works (he's very much at the subtle end of this spectrum). If you find such shenanigans entertaining, I can suggest some books that help with finding Germanic-origin words instead of French/Latin/etc-origin ones.

Secondly, a question: in your research/experience, have you noticed the Five Rites leading to more weight loss than a comparable amount of physical exercise? Details below, in case they're helpful (to avoid a second comment if needed, but easily skippable if not).

Details: At some point late last year/early this year, I started doing the Five Rites as my main physical practice. I began with 3 repetitions of each rite and worked my way up as described in The Eye of Revelation to the full 21 repetitions per rite. At some point, I began noticing numbness/tingling in my forearms/wrists/hands, and I wasn't sure if the 5 Rites were contributing, so I cut way way back, and for many months did 9 repetitions of each rite 2-3 times a week. During this time period, I didn't pay particularly close attention to my diet, but over ~6 months, I lost about 15 pounds (I could afford it, but I wasn't especially overweight to begin with). This is by no means a complaint - I feel and look good, and I haven't been/am not stressed about my weight the way I have been at times in the past. I'm just a bit perplexed what happened.

Thanks very much for your time and this space,
Jeff

replies.content:
I would also be interested to hear JMG's take here, but let me share some other information that might be relevant. I didn't do a controlled experiment where I held all variables steady except one, instead, I took more of the spaghetti approach: throw it all at the wall at once and see what sticks:

1) The symptoms I was experiencing were consistent with pre-carpal tunnel issues, so that guided what kinds of things I looked for and tried to change
2) Like many folks these days, I type a lot and am not as careful with my form/posture as I could be, so I've tried to improve on that
3) Around the same time, I was taking up more writing and journaling by hand, which meant holding and using a pen more than I had in year - I noticed some specific pain in the first knuckle of my pointer finger in my writing hand. For 2) and 3), I started doing more regular stretches of the muscles and tendons in my forearms (stretch 1: hold hand straight out, put palm outward, like saying "stop!", pull back towards body on fingers with other hand. Stretch 2: with and still out, pull fingers down and back, so back of hand is facing up/outward)
4) I tried to become more aware of times I was resting my elbows or forearms on hard surfaces and to do less of that. One that had become pretty regular was the edge of the tub when I give my daughter a bath. I now try to sit up and not rest my arms there
5) I was doing other exercises at the same time, including heavy deadlifts and Indian Club exercises. When I started having the issues, I stopped doing those for several months, and now I'm considering cautiously re-introducing some other stuff after I work my way back up to doing 21 reps of each rite every day
6) I usually sleep either like a vampire (hands crossed on my chest laying on my back) or like a toddler (laying on my stomach with arms under my pillow). I've tried to default to sleeping on my back with my arms straight at my sides
7) While doing the Five Rites themselves, I made a couple of changes to my form that I think are indicated by the descriptions and diagrams in The Eye of Revelation, but that I wasn't doing in my initial try. On the third rite, I made sure to lower my head to the front at the end of the motion, and on the fourth rite, I started being more gentle about coming to full extension, rather than "popping" up, and at the end of the motion, I lower my head to the front and make sure to relax my arms, bending the elbows

So, you can see that I was far from sure that it was the Five Rites themselves causing the numbness, but it's something I'm going to keep an eye out for as I ramp back up, and I can share here if I notice it again.

Cheers,
Jeff

Not "don't use the Latinate words", but... if you want to be perceived as honest, forthright, and down-to-earth, rather than pretentious snooty and academic (personal essay or creative writing for the public vs. academic writing for other academics), never use a five-dollar Latin-derived word when a good Germanic word will do. If you exhume the decomposing corpse, you are distancing yourself from the subject you're writing about: we can see you holding a white hanky to your nose. You're squeamish. If that's the effect you want, great! If not... consider digging up his rotting body instead. Now you're fully engaged with your subject and you're not afraid to get your hands dirty. What part of your reader are you trying to reach? Latin is the way to his head. German is the way to his gut.

Hey JMG

A fortnight ago we were talking about why heptagons aren't common in geometry, sacred or otherwise, and you mentioned that accuracy is important in sacred geometry ,I wanted to know why but I asked you too late.
Anyhow, why exactly is accuracy so important in sacred geometry?

replies.title: Re: Sacred geometry
replies.content: There are a very few constructions in sacred geometry that can't be done perfectly with straightedge and compass -- squaring the circle is the one many people have heard of, and they're important for complex symbolic reasons. Other than those, it's essential in sacred geometry that you get it right. Partly that's because sacred geometry was used in ancient times to build temples and the like, and precision was important in those to produce resonating spaces for subtle energies; partly, the exact forms have resonances in consciousness that the inexact simply don't have, and so being able to get it right when you're going to be meditating on the result is important.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dream interpretation is always highly personal, and I am in no way a professional, so take the below with as much salt as seems appropriate.

The core elements seem to be 1) guilt over doing something wrong, 2) close links to the victims, and 3) feelings of suspicion/dislike/blame. The straightforward interpretation would be that you did something about which you feel guilty, and you worry that it affects how other people see you/think of you. One possibility that comes to mind is that the "murder" might have been of some part of yourself - often in dreams, everyone involved is in some important sense a part of you. Is there some aspect of yourself that you had to shut down/get rid of/cut out of your life? The identities of the victim(s) might give some clues here. For example, if it was a woman, it might be some aspect of the feminine side of your personality, or if a child, your innocence/playfulness, and so forth.

Another possibility: is there something that rationally you know was justifiable, but might still make you feel a little weird? Sometimes dreams amplify the intensity/drama of something to call attention to it. Maybe you were in a bad relationship and had to cut someone out of your life and it was the right call, but you've got some lingering feelings on the matter to sort through, or you suspect the folks around you don't understand why it was needed.

A book that I've found enormously useful for dream interpretation is Dreams, a Portal to the Source by Edward C. Whitmont and Sylvia Brinton Perera. It has a lot of tips on how to interpret some of the specific elements of dreams, rather than only the broad themes or storylines.

Hope this helps a bit, and good luck sorting things out,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Holy Languages
replies.content:
Harry Dresden, fictional wizard in The Dresden Files*, has a theory.

In a language that you know all the words have multiple contexts and nuances of meaning. But in a language that you don't know they can be assigned one exact meaning. Using a foreign or dead language that you don't know allows you to make the mental patterns shaped by the words exactly match the pattern that you intend to create.

replies.content:
I was considering asking you and the commentary it here about something that's come up for me lately related to German heritage in German culture. Then I changed my mind, but seeing these comments feels too synchronicitous to ignore.

A couple of years back, my younger brother got a DNA test. The results were more or less what I expected -- a little over 40% of our DNA comes from various parts of Germany, another 40 plus percent comes from different parts of the British Isles, mainly Ireland and Scotland, and the rest is Italian. So far so good.

Then an odd thing happened. Every year or two my brother's DNA test-- he has an app which links to it on his phone-- will update. And every time it updates, it reduces the amount of heritage given to every other group, and increases the Germans. At this point it is showing us as 85% German, which is not possible. My brother, a lover of technology and what they call science, updates his personal identity accordingly. It doesn't really matter that he knew his Italian grandfather until the latter's death in 2012, we are now apparently only 6% Italian, because the telephone says so.

Now I found all of this very annoying, and so I spent a fair bit of time researching our ancestry. No, we are not 85% German, far from it. But we are a little over half German, which is still rather odd as we were never raised to think of ourselves as German in any way. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: If I wanted to undergo Jungian therapy in a DIY way, the available tools are journalling, a close reading of his books, and active imagination, is that right? I believe Jung only wrote books tailored to the trained therapist aside from Man and His Symbols, which isn't really a manual.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It sounds like you've got the basic toolkit right there, but if I might share a few resources you might find helpful:

1) Some of Jung's students were a bit more approachable and easier to read at a beginner level than the man himself. You might take a look at The Origins and History of Consciousness by Eric Neumann for a general theoretical overview that helps make sense of the Jungian view, and Marie Louise von Franz has rather a lot of works, many of which focus especially on the feminine - her book on alchemy is highly recommended and might be of interest to someone in this space, but I haven't read it myself.

2) I'd consider some dream journaling and interpretation as a potentially useful tool as well. Dreams, A Portal to the Source by Edward C. Whitmont and Sylvia Brinton Perera is very firmly in the Jungian tradition, thorough, and gives lots of practical advice on applying what's in it to actual patients (including yourself!)

3) Some more recent folks who have been heavily influenced by Jung have been helpful to me in better understanding his work. Jordan B. Peterson is heavily influenced by Jung and has been a doorway into understanding Jung's work and theories for me. If you're looking for something more theoretical, Maps of Meaning is an excellent breakdown of the idea of archetypes and some of the underlying dynamics within myths, but it's fairly dense (not as tough as Jung himself, but not easy going either). 12 Rules for Life is much more focused on practical "self help" style stuff and is much easier reading, but it's less explicitly linked with Jung. If you are a man or trying to understand men in your life, Roger Moore's King, Warrior, Magician, Lover is a helpful look at the role those four archetypes play in masculinity and what they look like when undeveloped or if their shadow side is manifesting. Owning Your Own Shadow by Robert A. Johnson is a straightforward, if somewhat shallow, approach to the Shadow archetype.

4) Lastly, a bit of a supplement to the "close reading of Jung's books" bit: one of the things I have found makes reading Jung's work so tough is how interconnected all of his thinking is. Another is that his thinking changed and developed over time! One thing that helps with both of these is to come back to stuff you've already read after you've read more widely. For example, reading The Archetypes and the Unconscious or Synchronicity early will be helpful in laying groundwork for reading other stuff down the line, but likely confusing. After you read a few other works, if you come back to either, you'll get more out of it and it will be less confusing. I suspect this holds true for many repetitions.

Hope these help, and good luck on your journey!

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thank you
replies.content:
1) One of the really unfortunate things about the way karma has been understood in popular culture is that it's been mixed up with all this stuff about blaming and deserving. That misses the point completely. The law of karma is simply that actions have consequences, for good or ill, in this life or another, and that what happens to us in each life has its roots in our actions in previous lives; this is how we learn not to do stupid and hurtful things. We've all done stupid and hurtful things, after all, in this life and other lives, and all those have consequences. Your friend who was sexually abused may well have abused someone else half a dozen lifetimes ago, and going through that wretched experience herself is part of the process of outgrowing the kind of thinking that makes such actions seem to make sense, to the perpetrators thereof. Again, we all go through such experiencs.

As for the people who do the stupid and hurtful things, they also have a choice to make. Let's say you have karma that will result at some point in getting beaten up unfairly. Any number of people could do that to you, and quite a few of them will have the chance to do it. Nobody has to do it, but sooner or later someone's going to give in to their worse side and start throwing punches. They get the karmic burden of that choice. So your cousin is storing up a great big heap of misery for himself for future lives, and the process of dealing with that will teach him not to be such a jerk.

comments.title: Narcissism
comments.content:
Would you kindly point me in the right direction towards a magical understanding of narcissism? My understanding had shifted over the years and lately I've been thinking about what exactly it is.

All children are born narcissists because survival depends on it. It doesn't matter if mom has a migraine, if you are hungry you will scream. Somewhere along the way, humans learn to empathize, see the other as other, and come to consider the other in their choice of what to say and do. There are various theories as to why some humans don't grow out of this early stage, largely blaming parents and largely focusing on trauma of some sort.

It seemed to me that it is a refusal to accept responsibility for one's internal and external needs, and to choose to remain a child, demanding that others fulfill their needs. It also seemed to me that the victim of a narcissist (excluding children) is also shirking responsibility in that they are not taking care of themselves and their own well-being, not having the courage to set boundaries, thus allowing the narcissist to harm them.

However, work with the Ogham fews suggested that the predatory nature of narcissism is left out of this understanding, and that by focusing on responsibility for oneself, I fail to grasp the full picture of what's going on. It also fails to account for karma brought in from previous incarnations.

I'm torn between two possible explanations of those who are born to narcissists and/or are plagued by them: 1. They were narcissists in a previous life and need to feel what a dumb idea that is, thus working off some karma. 2. They were wusses who failed to learn to stand up for themselves in a previous life and they need to keep trying until they learn to do so. The bullies will show up until they do. 3. Possibly there is a third option I haven't thought of yet.

If our basic character continues from incarnation to incarnation, as I believe you suggested at some point, it would be more likely that a narcissist would come back in conditions that would allow that basic character trait to be manifested again, rather than the complete opposite (the wuss who can't set boundaries AKA Myriam, especially with loved ones). The karma of the narcissist would be the pain they are in, perhaps? The need to struggle past the narcissism?

If there is one theme that runs through my life it is narcissism, in all it's possibilities: mother, sister, husband, daughter***, friend, neighbours, and even an elderly woman I took care of, and seeing the effect on all the other loved ones in the larger circles. Their narcissism is not just my assessment, (always checking myself), it is almost unanimous agreement among those of my larger circles affected by them. I asked the fews if I had been a narcissist in my previous life, and significantly, I got the blank few. I suspect the possibility that I am not ready to know what happened because I still have some learning to do, and knowing whether I was a narcissist or a wuss would interfere with the lessons. Or maybe for some other reason I didn't get.

It seems to me at this time that in this life my task is to learn to set boundaries without cutting people off or dismissing them, encourage them to take responsibility for their problems rather than help them, and to me very significantly, REMAIN RESPECTFUL. Allow the other to walk whatever journey they must go on as narcissists. comments.title: Started sobbing in a ruined abbey
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Recently, my partner and I went on a hiking holiday in England. On the 2nd of June our hike happened to pass by Shap Abbey, a mile from the village of Shap in the Lake District. The abbey, established in 1199 and closed in 1540, has been the home of a community of Premonstratensian monks for about 340 years. The whole building is currently in ruin, with only the impressive clock tower and an outline of foot-high walls still standing.

Solemnly walking down the abbey's "chambers" and "halls" (i.e. the spaces in between the outlines of the walls) I looked around and tried to imagine what these spaces must have looked like in their glory days, when the walls and ceilings were still standing. As I stepped into the courtyard of the abbey, I was suddenly overwhelmed by a dizzying feeling. A wave of sadness washed over me and I started sobbing uncontrollably. I couldn't stop my tears although I really tried, embarrassed to cry before the other visitors; they probably wondered what the hell was going on with me. In fact, I was wondering what the hell was going on with me! I can only describe it as a sudden but deep feeling of connection with the abbey, as if I had 'lived there before'. This caused an excruciating sadness and desperation - might I say anger? - at the state that it was currently in. 'How could they have let this happen?', was a thought that came to me multiple times.

I walked towards my partner and we sat down, so I could have a proper cry away from all the visitors. We were both stunned by what had just happened. I'm in my thirties and well traveled. I have seen many old churches and ruined buildings in my life, but I have never shed a tear over any of them. But here it felt as if 'I had been there' in its glory days; that somehow this place had meant the world to me. I felt so confused and disoriented that 'it was all gone', and at the same time my "normal self" was wondering why I was having these strong feelings about a place I had never been in my life!

So finally, my questions are:
Question 1:
How would you judge this experience?

I figure that it could have (roughly) one of two explanations:
Explanation A)
This abbey has been used for prayer and contemplation for 340 years, so what I sensed was just a strong connection with the remaining energy of this sacred place. However, that would not explain why I started sobbing uncontrollably at this specific abbey, and not at any of the other places of prayer I have visited in my life.

Or:
Explanation B)
This specific abbey (or a very similar one) is somehow connected to me personally, or one of my past selves. I was born in The Netherlands, although for reasons I cannot necessarily explain I have always felt a strong connection with England and English culture. I have been there multiple times, although never been at this specific location. So, I am open to the idea that I might have lived in an English monastery in a previous life.

Question 2:
If you think explanation B is more likely, then what could I do with this?
My partner tells me that your opinion on past lives is something like: 'Don't go chasing after your past life experiences, they will come to you when you need them'. Well, this experience definitely came to me in a pretty strong way, which might be because it has something meaningful to say to me. The question then is: how can I find out what it is saying?

Thank you so much for providing this space!

Athena

replies.title: Re: Started sobbing in a ruined abbey
replies.content:
1) I wouldn't judge this experience at all, since it wasn't my experience! I'd listen, run my hand down my beard, and say, "Hmm! That's most interesting."

2) I'd encourage you to try doing journaling on the subject. Ask yourself, "what does this experience have to teach me?" and then write down whatever comes to mind, without editing or censoring anything. Do this repeatedly and see where it takes you.

replies.title: Re: Magic Monday queries
replies.content:
1) This is one of the reasons I don't generally use planetary return charts. Most outer planet returns include this little feature -- my Saturn return a little while ago, for example, had that threefold pattern. I find it more useful simply to do an annual progression for the year(s) in which the return takes place, and leave it at that.

2) Try to figure out why the reading is ambiguous and ask a different question. In my experience, at least, ambiguous readings mean that I've asked the wrong question or phrased it in an unhelpful way.

3) My recommendation with such things is to write down what you remember, in as much detail as you can, and then set them aside for a while. If memories of the same life keep coming up with increasing detail, yes, you've probably got actual memories. Yes, if the lives you recall were troubled, the ones before then planted the seeds of the trouble. You'll remember those, too, in due time; it may not be fun.

comments.title: Second book of the Corpus Hermeticum
comments.content:
Do you have any opinion on the end of the second book of the Corpus Hermeticum, where the dialogue ends with Hermes saying that people without children will "suffer punishment after death from the divine powers": "the soul without children is condemned to a body that is neither male or female, and is cursed by the sun. Therefore, Asclepius, do not congratulate anyone without children bur rather take pity on his misfortune, knowing what punishment awaits him."

I finally got around to reading through the Corpus Hermeticium. I had actually heard about this passage online before, but the vehemence of it struck me kind of hard, as I have not had any children (as of yet anyways). It is a sore spot for me psychologically, I only ever got to dating a bit later in life, and I'm not sure it will be possible for me and my partner to have any children.

I realize it's an old text, written in a very different culture. I think the passage also struck me as, though I'm familiar with Jewish and Islamic traditions that place a lot of emphasis on a person creating a family, most of my reading has been in Christian monasticism, Buddhism, and Hindu traditions like Advaita, which don't seem to place as much emphasis on the spiritual importance of having started a family.

Anyways, just wondering if you had any thoughts on that passage from the Corpus Hermeticum, or more generally on topic. Thank you

replies.title: Re: Second book of the Corpus Hermeticum
replies.content: Er, the Corpus Hermeticum is not a set of sacred scriptures. It's a collection of the insights and opinions of people who lived in a very different culture and age -- a time, to be precise, when depopulation due to plunging birth rates was becoming a major social problem. Keep that in mind, and never let a book do your thinking for you.

replies.title: Re: Second book of the Corpus Hermeticum
replies.content:
Thank you for the reply. I hadn't assumed that the Corpus Hermeticum was comprised of infallible sacred scriptures. I guess I'd assumed that it was like any other sacred writing (i.e. a book of the Bible, or say an Upanishad) it could have some relevant insights into the spiritual life and the progress of a soul, mixed among more immediate concerns of that time and place (I'm thinking about 2nd century C.E. Alexandria.)

Is that basically how you'd see the Corpus Hermeticum? Similar to, say, the books of the Bible, or the Upanishads, or the Chaldean Oracle? Not to be ascribed to, wholesale, in a fundamentalist sense, but something to be considered, as tradition recommends it to us for consideration?

Also, I guess I never really believed that the progress of the soul after death was something that could be understood by taking thought, I assumed that any knowledge we would have about the process could mainly be gleaned from some sort of revelation (but maybe brought on by methods like discursive meditation.)

Just by thinking about it rationally, I could see that condemning childless people to torments would consign a great many seemingly blameless people to punishments they don't seem to deserve. But just in terms of thinking about it, just because it doesn't seem just to me, it doesn't seem obvious that the afterlife process might not work that way. Many of the descriptions of the judgement process in the afterlife seem somewhat unjust, to my limited understanding.

Anyways, I was more just interested if you had any thoughts on why that passage was added (which I take your answer to be that it was related to the demographic pressures of that time) but also was wondering if you thought that a person being a parent or not was especially relevant in the afterlife process?

replies.title: Re: Second book of the Corpus Hermeticum
replies.content: I treat the Corpus Hermeticum as a set of interesting reflections worth thinking about, yes. As for parenthood or the lack of same, as far as I can see, it depends wholly on the individual karma of the person in question. replies.title: Re: Solomonic Magic vs. Goetia
replies.content: That's certainly my take on things. replies.title: Re: Aboriginal magic
replies.content:
Definitely not an area of expertise, here, as I am an American and have only done very limited reading on Australia and her aboriginal peoples. One thought that comes to mind, though, is that one aspect of what might be going on besides what JMG said is that "magic" may be considered "bad branding" amongst those folks who are concerned about the power/status/perception of aboriginal peoples in a world dominated by atheistic modernist materialism. Especially since traditional beliefs and practices were so long labeled as backwards, primitive, or worse, by the settler culture.

One of the few books I have read on the subject is Sand Talk by Tyson Yunkaporta, which I very much enjoyed and found useful. It seems to show a little bit of both - there's a chapter where he talks about burning some herbs to exorcise a troublesome ghost, but the entire thrust of the book is about the kind of thinking and understanding his culture has that is missing from the Western worldview, and tries to justify much of that in more useful-to-the-outside-world terms.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Aboriginal magic
replies.content:
As an Australian, no, that's completely incorrect. They are swimming in magic, and I wouldn't doubt that they have something on every single object covering every scrap of the entire continent. Also, they are one of the groups that habitually use malefic magic, and have done so for a very long time.

They will never tell you a single scrap of information about any of it. They are still at war with the White man. They are also at war with the migrants that come to replace the White man. Welcome to Country ceremony == When are you Leaving? ceremony.

replies.title: Re: Aboriginal magic
replies.content:
Person of aboriginal descent here, the only way you could get this impression is reading books written by westerners thinking in a western paradigm. 'Magic' is everywhere through aboriginal culture, it's just not explicit because as others have mentioned there is not the same level of separation between the physical plane and other planes as the modern west.

The 'animist' (problematic word but useful) way of doing things is just a very different way to view the world, so it is hard to grasp unless you go and spend some extended time with aboriginal people who still practice the old ways.

Yunkaporta's book, as JP Russell mentioned, is a good place to start.

comments.content: Does Neptune Retrograde mean widespread nostalgia? Individual nostalgia?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That depends on what all the other planets in the heavens are doing. One rule that anyone interested in astrology needs to brutally burn into their backsides with a branding iron is this: Every factor in astrology must be read in the context of the whole chart. Neptune retrograde can mean any of 5760 different things -- that's because it can be in any of 12 signs, any of 12 houses, and in aspect to any of 8 other planets by any of 5 different aspects. If this sounds complicated, why, yes, it is!

replies.title: Combinations
replies.content:
It's even worse than that -- it'd be 12*12*6^8 or about 242 million. All the more reason to avoid those quick rules of thumb that planet X doing Y means Z.


I'm assuming here that the "5" is the number of possible aspects, not counting "no aspect," so that's how it ends up at 6. If the 5 already includes the possibility of no aspect, then it's a mere 56 million.

Also, if you want to consider the possibility of retrogrades for 7 planets too, that scales everything up by another factor of 2^7 = 128.

In other words, just say no to cookie-cutter astrology.

comments.title: Journaling and Anger
comments.content:
I am dealing with a lot of anger; we recently moved and I didn't want to. (PS- Your theory that depression dulls anger feels tangibly real. It's a huge, heavy, damp and stuffy blanket over it.)

replies.title: Re: Journaling and Anger
replies.content: Anger, according to the teachings of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, is usually a secondary emotion -- we feel angry because that's less miserable than feeling the primary emotion, which is usually fear, grief, or shame. That's why you can't get rid of anger by venting it. You have to work down to the primary emotion and release that, and then the anger will go away. With that in mind, you might try journaling not to release anger but to find the underlying tangle of emotions that drive the anger.
